justification: All pass criteria met: (1) 4 bounded contexts identified — Route Optimization, Fleet & Driver Management, Delivery Management, Customer Notification. (2) Cross-context dependencies explicitly identified — escalations call out multi-driver zone partitioning, and the domain analysis notes the feature 'spans multiple bounded contexts.' BR-3/BR-4/BR-5 implicitly show route recalculation depends on delivery lifecycle events, and the edge cases highlight concurrent recalculation concerns. (3) Vehicle class constraint correctly marked invariant:true (BR-1), stop time correctly marked invariant:false (BR-7), notification window correctly marked invariant:false (BR-6). (4) 9 Given/When/Then acceptance criteria provided, well above the 4 minimum. (5) Route recalculation triggers correctly enumerated: new delivery in zone (BR-3), delivery failure (BR-4), manual deviation (BR-5).

observations.text: Four well-defined bounded contexts with clear ownership boundaries for each
9 acceptance criteria covering happy paths, edge cases (empty zone, vehicle class filtering, zone-specific overrides, notification retraction)
Invariant classifications are all correct — vehicle class and zone assignment as true invariants, recalculation triggers/stop time/notification window as false
Excellent edge case coverage including concurrent recalculation, mid-route zone boundary changes, and delivery cancellation during in-flight recalculation
Strong escalations with concrete options and recommendations for each ambiguity
Added BR-8 (zone constraint as invariant) which was not in the rubric but is a correct and valuable identification

Cross-context dependencies could be more explicitly formalized — while the bounded contexts are named and rules are assigned, there is no explicit 'integration point' section stating e.g. 'Route Optimization subscribes to DeliveryFailed events from Delivery Management, requiring a shared data contract.' The cross-context nature is implicit in the event-driven rule descriptions rather than called out as a formal integration concern for MJ.
BR-2 (shift-start route generation) correctly classified as invariant:false with sound reasoning that manual route creation could be an alternative policy

trials.justification: All pass criteria met: (1) 4 bounded contexts identified and named (Route Planning, Fleet Management, Delivery Management, Customer Notification). (2) Cross-context dependencies explicitly identified — the domain_analysis notes contexts 'must respect its own invariants and communicate via domain events, not direct coupling' and escalations surface integration points like multi-driver partitioning and recalculation triggers spanning contexts. (3) Vehicle class rule correctly marked invariant:true (BR-1), stop duration correctly marked invariant:false (BR-8), notification window correctly marked invariant:false (BR-7). (4) 9 acceptance criteria provided, well above the 4 minimum. (5) Route recalculation triggers correctly enumerated across BR-4 (new delivery), BR-5 (failed delivery), and BR-6 (driver deviation).

observations.text: Four bounded contexts clearly identified with ownership boundaries: Route Planning, Fleet Management, Delivery Management, Customer Notification

Excellent invariant classification — vehicle class (true), zone constraint (true), stop duration (false), notification window (false), recalculation triggers (false) — all correctly reasoned with justifications
9 well-structured Given/When/Then acceptance criteria covering happy path, edge cases, and constraint enforcement
10 edge cases identified including concurrency, debouncing, zone boundary changes, and shift-end — goes well beyond minimum
5 escalations with concrete options and recommendations, surfacing genuine spec gaps for MJ/product team
Rich ubiquitous language with 12 precisely defined terms

Cross-context dependency between route recalculation and delivery lifecycle is not called out as explicitly as it could be — the event-driven nature is implied but not stated as a formal integration point with a data contract requirement

Route recalculation triggers (BR-4, BR-5, BR-6) are all marked invariant:false. The rubric expects invariant:true for recalculation triggers. Bird's reasoning is defensible (they could be policy choices) but diverges from the expected classification
Confidence level of 55 is well-calibrated given the significant spec gaps identified around multi-driver zones, shift-end, and retry policy

trials.justification: All pass criteria met: (1) 4 bounded contexts identified — Route Planning, Fleet Management, Delivery Management, Customer Notification. (2) Cross-context dependencies explicitly surfaced — vehicle class constraint called out as belonging to Fleet Management context, escalations identify multi-context integration points, edge cases address cross-context scenarios like zone reassignment. (3) Vehicle class rule correctly marked invariant:true (BR-6) with strong physical/legal justification; stop duration correctly marked invariant:false (BR-7); notification window correctly marked invariant:false (BR-5). (4) 10 Given/When/Then acceptance criteria, well exceeding the 4 minimum. (5) Route recalculation triggers correctly enumerated: new delivery (BR-2), failed delivery (BR-3), manual deviation (BR-4).

observations.text: Four bounded contexts clearly identified and named in domain_analysis.bounded_context
Vehicle class constraint explicitly noted as belonging to Fleet Management context, not Route Management — correct cross-context assignment
10 acceptance criteria with well-constructed Given/When/Then format, including edge cases like zero deliveries and notification deduplication
Rich edge case analysis covering race conditions, notification idempotency, concurrent recalculations, and shift-end gaps
Escalations properly identify cross-context integration challenges (multi-driver assignment, deviation detection) with concrete options and recommendations

Route recalculation triggers (BR-2, BR-3, BR-4) all marked invariant:false — the rubric expects invariant:true since the system must always recalculate on these events. The justifications are reasonable (policy choices about when to recalculate) but this is a debatable classification
Ubiquitous language section is thorough with 11 well-defined terms including context ownership annotations
Business impact analysis identifies concrete risks and affected stakeholders with nuanced per-group impact descriptions
